document.source_title: Practice Makes Deterrence: India’s Next Nuclear Challenge at Sea
document.author: Kunal Singh
document.summary: India's entry into the nuclear-powered submarine domain with the INS Aridhaman on April 3, 2026, marks a significant milestone in its nuclear deterrent capabilities. With three operational ballistic missile submarines, India can maintain a continuous at-sea deterrent. However, challenges such as civil-military relations, varying submarine capabilities, and limitations in missile range must be addressed to fully leverage this strategic asset.
key_takeaways: INS Aridhaman enhances India's nuclear deterrent capabilities.
India can now maintain a continuous at-sea nuclear deterrent.
Challenges include civil-military relations and submarine capability disparities.
India's missile range limitations affect its deterrent posture.
The need for nuclear attack submarines to protect boomers is critical.
Operational readiness and doctrinal innovation are essential for effective deterrence.
document.why_it_matters: This analysis highlights the strategic implications of India's nuclear submarine capabilities and the necessary reforms to enhance its deterrent posture, which is crucial for regional stability and security.

claims.claim_text: India can now reliably maintain at least one ballistic missile submarine on deterrent patrol.
claims.evidence_text: With three boomers, India can now reliably always maintain at least one on deterrent patrol.

claims.claim_text: India's ballistic missile submarines vary in size and capabilities.
claims.evidence_text: The INS Arihant and INS Arighat both displace 6,000 tons, while Aridhaman displaces 7,000 tons.

claims.claim_text: India's K-4 missile range is limited and affects its deterrent capabilities.
claims.evidence_text: The K-4 must be launched only from the northernmost parts of the Bay of Bengal, thereby potentially revealing the submarine’s location.

entities.name: INS Aridhaman
entities.description: India's third ballistic missile-carrying, nuclear-powered submarine.
entities.evidence_text: India’s third ballistic missile-carrying, nuclear-powered submarine — the INS Aridhaman — entered service on April 3, 2026.

entities.name: K-4
entities.description: A sea-launched ballistic missile with a range of 3,500 kilometers.
entities.evidence_text: Arihant and Arighat can carry either 12 K-15 missiles with a range of 750 kilometers or four K-4 missiles with a range of 3,500 kilometers.

events.evidence_text: When India’s third ballistic missile-carrying, nuclear-powered submarine — the INS Aridhaman — entered service on April 3, 2026.

citations.title: Practice Makes Deterrence: India’s Next Nuclear Challenge at Sea

source_credibility.reason: Published by War on the Rocks, a reputable source on military and security issues.
